From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Alloys with high proportions of several metals

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Atomic_structure_model_of_fcc_CoCrFeMnNi.png/250px-Atomic_structure_model_of_fcc_CoCrFeMnNi.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Atomic_structure_model_of_fcc_CoCrFeMnNi.png)

Atomic structure model of fcc CoCrFeMnNi[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Wang1-1)

**High-entropy alloys** (**HEAs**) are [alloys](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alloy "Alloy") that are formed by mixing equal or relatively large proportions of (usually) five or more [elements](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element"). Prior to the synthesis of these substances, typical [metal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metal "Metal") alloys comprised one or two major components with smaller amounts of other elements. For example, additional elements can be added to [iron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iron "Iron") to improve its properties, thereby creating an iron-based alloy, but typically in fairly low proportions, such as the proportions of [carbon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon "Carbon"), [manganese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manganese "Manganese"), and others in various [steels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Steel "Steel").[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2) Hence, high-entropy alloys are a novel class of materials.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Wang1-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2) The term "high-entropy alloys" was coined by [Taiwanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") scientist [Jien-Wei Yeh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jein-Wei_Yeh "Jein-Wei Yeh")[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-3) because the [entropy increase of mixing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entropy_of_mixing "Entropy of mixing") is substantially higher when there is a larger number of elements in the mix, and their proportions are more nearly equal.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-4) Some alternative names, such as multi-component alloys, compositionally complex alloys and multi-principal-element alloys are also suggested by other researchers.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:9-6) Compositionally complex alloys (CCAs) are an up-and-coming group of materials due to their unique mechanical properties. They have high strength and toughness, the ability to operate at higher temperatures than current alloys, and have superior ductility. Material ductility is important because it quantifies the permanent deformation a material can withstand before failure, a key consideration in designing safe and reliable materials. Due to their enhanced properties, CCAs show promise in extreme environments. An extreme environment presents significant challenges for a material to perform to its intended use within designated safety limits. CCAs can be used in several applications such as aerospace propulsion systems, land-based gas turbines, heat exchangers, and the chemical process industry.

These alloys are currently the focus of significant attention in [materials science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science") and engineering because they have potentially desirable properties.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2) Furthermore, research indicates that some HEAs have considerably better [strength-to-weight ratios](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strength-to-weight_ratio "Strength-to-weight ratio"), with a higher degree of [fracture resistance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fracture_mechanics "Fracture mechanics"), [tensile strength](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensile_strength "Tensile strength"), and [corrosion](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corrosion "Corrosion") and [oxidation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oxidation "Oxidation") resistance than conventional alloys.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-8)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-9) Although HEAs have been studied since the 1980s, research substantially accelerated in the 2010s.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-tsai-2)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:9-6)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-lavine-10)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-shipman-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Youssef-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-cryogenic-13)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-gludov-14)

Development
-----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Development")]

Although HEAs were considered from a theoretical standpoint as early as 1981[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-15) and 1996,[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-16) and throughout the 1980s, in 1995 [Taiwanese](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") scientist [Jien-Wei Yeh](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jein-Wei_Yeh "Jein-Wei Yeh") came up with his idea for ways of actually creating high-entropy alloys, while driving through the [Hsinchu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hsinchu "Hsinchu"), Taiwan, countryside. Soon after, he decided to begin creating these special alloys in his lab, being in the only region researching these alloys for over a decade. Most countries in [Europe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Europe "Europe"), the [United States](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States "United States"), and other parts of the world lagged behind in the development of HEAs. Significant research interest from other countries did not develop until after 2004 when Yeh and his team of scientists built the world's first high-entropy alloys to withstand extremely high temperatures and pressures.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-17) Potential applications include use in state-of-the-art race cars, spacecraft, submarines, nuclear reactors,[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Sonal1-18) jet aircraft, nuclear weapons, long range [hypersonic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypersonic "Hypersonic")[missiles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hsiung_Feng_III "Hsiung Feng III"), and so on.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-20)

A few months later, after the publication of Yeh's paper, another independent paper on high-entropy alloys was published by a team from the [United Kingdom](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") composed of [Brian Cantor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brian_Cantor "Brian Cantor"), I. T. H. Chang, P. Knight, and A. J. B. Vincent. Yeh was also the first to coin the term "high-entropy alloy" when he attributed the high configurational entropy as the mechanism stabilizing the [solid solution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_solution "Solid solution") phase.[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Yeh-AEM_2004-21) Cantor did the first work in the field in the late 1970s and early 1980s, though he did not publish until 2004. Unaware of Yeh's work, he did not describe his new materials as "high-entropy" alloys, preferring the term "multicomponent alloys". The base alloy he developed, equiatomic CrMnFeCoNi, has been the subject of considerable work in the field, and is known as the "Cantor alloy", with similar derivatives known as Cantor alloys.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Cantor_2004-22) It was one of the first HEAs to be reported to form a single-phase FCC ([face-centred cubic crystal structure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cubic_crystal_system "Cubic crystal system")) solid solution.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-23)

Before the classification of high-entropy alloys and multi-component systems as a separate class of materials, nuclear scientists had already studied a system that can now be classified as a high-entropy alloy: within [nuclear fuels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nuclear_fuel "Nuclear fuel") Mo-Pd-Rh-Ru-Tc particles form at grain boundaries and at fission gas bubbles.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Middleburgh_2015-24) Understanding the behavior of these "five-metal particles" was of specific interest to the medical industry because [Tc-99m](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Technetium-99m "Technetium-99m") is an important [medical imaging](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medical_imaging "Medical imaging") isotope.

Definition
----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Definition")]

There is no universally agreed-upon definition of a HEA. The originally defined HEAs as alloys containing at least 5 elements with concentrations between 5 and 35 [atomic percent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atomic_percent "Atomic percent").[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Yeh-AEM_2004-21) Later research however, suggested that this definition could be expanded. Otto et al. suggested that only alloys that form a solid solution with no [intermetallic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intermetallic "Intermetallic") phases should be considered true high-entropy alloys, because the formation of ordered phases decreases the entropy of the system.[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-enthalpy_effect-25) Some authors have described four-component alloys as high-entropy alloys[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Size-dependent_plasticity_in_an_Nb2-26) while others have suggested that alloys meeting the other requirements of HEAs, but with only 2–4 elements[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-zenodo.org-27) or a mixing entropy between [_R_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ideal_gas_constant "Ideal gas constant") and 1.5 _R_[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-28) should be considered "medium-entropy" alloys.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-zenodo.org-27)

The four core effects of HEAs
-----------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The four core effects of HEAs")]

Due to their multi-component composition, HEAs exhibit different basic effects than other traditional alloys that are based only on one or two elements. Those different effects are called "the four core effects of HEAs" and are behind a lot of the particular microstructure and properties of HEAs.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-29) The four core effects are high entropy, severe lattice distortion, sluggish diffusion, and cocktail effects.

### High entropy effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: High entropy effect")]

The high entropy effect is the most important effect because it can enhance the formation of solid solutions and makes the microstructure much simpler than expected. Prior knowledge expected multi component alloys to have many different interactions among elements and thus form many different kinds of binary, ternary, and quaternary compounds and/or segregated phases. Thus, such alloys would possess complicated structures, brittle by nature. This expectation in fact neglects the effect of high entropy. Indeed, according to the [second law of thermodynamics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Second_law_of_thermodynamics "Second law of thermodynamics"), the state having the lowest mixing [Gibbs free energy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy")Δ G m i x=Δ H m i x−T Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta G_{mix}=\Delta H_{mix}-T\Delta S_{mix}}![Image 5: {\displaystyle \Delta G_{mix}=\Delta H_{mix}-T\Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b38c4738d6a6fd6ee33583758e127a700154eb01) among all possible states would be the equilibrium state. Elemental phases based on one major element have small[enthalpy of mixing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enthalpy_of_mixing "Enthalpy of mixing") (Δ H m i x{\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}![Image 6: {\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/82ce3b8b3345b674fd7bfae037d37db7afa79ca0)) and a small [entropy of mixing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Entropy_of_mixing "Entropy of mixing") (Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}![Image 7: {\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/deec63413959f6306dcb4fa1810f907e111cfe2d)), and compound phases have large Δ H m i x{\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}![Image 8: {\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/82ce3b8b3345b674fd7bfae037d37db7afa79ca0)but small Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}![Image 9: {\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/deec63413959f6306dcb4fa1810f907e111cfe2d); on the other hand, solid-solution phases containing multiple elements have medium Δ H m i x{\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}![Image 10: {\displaystyle \Delta H_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/82ce3b8b3345b674fd7bfae037d37db7afa79ca0) and high Δ S m i x{\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}![Image 11: {\displaystyle \Delta S_{mix}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/deec63413959f6306dcb4fa1810f907e111cfe2d). As a result, solid-solution phases become highly competitive for equilibrium state and more stable especially at high temperatures.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:22-30)

### Severe lattice distortion effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Severe lattice distortion effect")]

[![Image 12](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Lattice_distortion_Effect.svg/365px-Lattice_distortion_Effect.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Lattice_distortion_Effect.svg)

Schematic diagram showing large lattice distortion existing in the five component BCC lattice

Because [solid solution](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solid_solution "Solid solution") phases with multi-principal elements are usually found in HEAs, the conventional crystal structure concept is thus extended from a one or two element basis to a multi-element basis. Every atom is surrounded by different kinds of atoms and thus suffers lattice strain and stress mainly due to atomic size difference. Besides the atomic size difference, both different bonding energy and crystal structure tendency among constituent elements are also believed to cause even higher lattice distortion because non-symmetrical bindings and electronic structure exist between an atom and its first neighbours. This distortion is believed to be the source of some of the mechanical, thermal, electrical, optical, and chemical behaviour of HEAs. Thus, overall lattice distortion would be more severe than that in traditional alloys in which most matrix atoms (or solvent atoms) have the same kind of atoms as their surroundings.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:22-30)

### Sluggish diffusion effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sluggish diffusion effect")]

As explained in the last section, an HEA mainly contains a random solid solution and/or an ordered solid solution. Their matrices could be regarded as whole-solute matrices. In HEAs, those whole-solute matrices' diffusion vacancies are surrounded by different element atoms, and thus have a specific lattice potential energy (LPE). This large fluctuation of LPE between lattice sites leads to low-LPE sites, serving as traps and hindering atomic diffusion.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-31) This leads to the sluggish diffusion effect.

### Cocktail effect

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cocktail effect")]

The cocktail effect is used to emphasise the enhancement of the alloy's properties by at least five major elements. Because HEAs might have one or more phases, the whole properties are from the overall contribution of the constituent phases. Besides, each phase is a solid solution and can be viewed as a composite with properties coming not only from the basic properties of the constituent, but by the mixture rule also from the interactions among all the constituents and from severe lattice distortion. The cocktail effect takes into account the effect from the atomic-scale multicomponent phases and from the multiple composite phases at the micro scale.[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-32)

Alloy design
------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-entropy_alloy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Alloy design")]

In conventional alloy design, one primary element such as iron, copper, or aluminum is chosen for its properties. Then, small amounts of additional elements are added to improve or add properties. Even among binary alloy systems, there are few common cases of both elements being used in nearly-equal proportions such as [Pb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lead "Lead")-[Sn](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tin "Tin")[solders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solder "Solder"). Therefore, much is known from experimental results about phases near the edges of [binary phase diagrams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_phase_diagram "Binary phase diagram") and the corners of [ternary phase diagrams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ternary_plot "Ternary plot") and much less is known about phases near the centers. In higher-order (4+ components) systems that cannot be easily represented on a two-dimensional phase diagram, virtually nothing is known.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-Cantor_2004-22)

Early research of HEA was focussed on forming single-phased solid solution, which could maximize the major features of high entropy alloy: high entropy, sluggish diffusion, severe lattice distortion, and cocktail effects. It has been pointed out that most successful materials need some secondary phase to strengthen the material,[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-33)[[34]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-34) and that any HEA used in application will have a multiphase microstructure.[[35]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-entropy_alloy#cite_note-:21-35) However, it is still important to form single-phased material since a single-phased sample is essential for understanding the underlying mechanism of HEAs and testing specific microstructures to find structures producing special properties.[[35]](https://en.wi

Abstract
--------

Modern engineering applications continually strive to develop greater performance mechanical components with good microstructural stability, improved mechanical properties, [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and decreased cost of repairing and maintenance. This necessitates the broad use of advanced high performance materials like [high entropy alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about high entropy alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HEAs). These alloys are created by combining five or more alloying elements in equal or substantial amount. About 5 to 35 at. % of the alloying element is present in HEAs. It is characterized primarily by greater entropy, slow diffusion, severe lattice distortion, and cocktail effects. Due to its advanced microstructural stability throughout a larger temperature span and for longer length of time, it demonstrates improved [mechanical characteristics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-property "Learn more about mechanical characteristics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") at ambient temperature, cryogenic temperature, and elevated temperature. The diversity of elemental contents and significantly higher mixing entropy of HEAs make them mechanically superior to classic metals and alloys. It also shows better [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to weight ratio. Hence, it qualifies as a possible structural and [functional material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/functional-material "Learn more about functional material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [aeroengine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aeroengine "Learn more about aeroengine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") applications. In this work, the studies on the HEAs are briefly reviewed. A basic explanation of the four core effects of HEAs is given. Discussion is held on microstructure and mechanical properties of HEAs. The processing routes for manufacturing of HEAs (arc melting, bridgman solidification, mechanical alloying and vapour deposition) are presented briefly. The influence of heat treatment on mechanical behavior and microstructure of HEAs is presented. The simulation approach of CALPHAD modeling for designing of HEAs is discussed briefly. The future scope for research and development of HEAs in aeroengine applications is briefed.

1. Introduction
---------------

Modern engineering applications continually strive to develop greater performance mechanical components with good microstructural stability, improved mechanical properties, [corrosion resistance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/corrosion-resistance "Learn more about corrosion resistance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and decreased cost of repairing and maintenance. This necessitates the broad use of advanced high-performance materials like high entropy alloys (HEAs). These alloys are created by combining five or more elements for alloying in equal or substantial amount. About 5 to 35 at. % of the alloying element is present in HEAs. It is characterized primarily by greater entropy, slow diffusion, severe lattice distortion, and cocktail effects [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0005), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0010), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0015), [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0020), [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0025). The diversity of elemental contents and significantly higher mixing entropy of HEAs make them mechanically superior to classic metals and alloys. Due to its advanced microstructural stability throughout a larger temperature and for longer length of time, it demonstrates improved mechanical capabilities at ambient temperature, cryogenic temperature, and elevated temperature. It shows outstanding resistance to erosion and corrosion, great [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and hardness, better strength to weight ratio and good ductility [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0030), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0035). Due to its excellent balance of exceptional fractured toughness and yielding strength, as demonstrated in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#f0005), HEAs have a greater threshold to breakage. Clearly, HEAs have a higher fracture toughness than the entirety of metals and alloys, but their yield strengths are comparable to [structural ceramics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/structural-ceramics "Learn more about structural ceramics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and certain bulk metallic glasses [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0040). Moreover, HEAs have been demonstrated to be oxidation- resistant at high temperatures, expanding their potential technological applications as potential substitutes for [Nickel alloys](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/nickel-alloys "Learn more about Nickel alloys from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in turbine systems [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0045). Hence, HEAs have grown to be the most practical choices for fulfilling the requirements for difficult loading condition, particularly in the fields of aerospace and power generation sectors [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0050). For structural, aeronautical, nuclear, and many other engineering fields, it is therefore a viable engineering material. It discovers utility for discharge nozzles, combustion chambers, and jet engine turbines.

Fig. 1. Comparison of fracture toughness and yielding [strength](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-strength "Learn more about strength from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of [engineering materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/engineering-material "Learn more about engineering materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with [HEAs](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/high-entropy-alloys "Learn more about HEAs from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0040).

During 2004, Cantor [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0055) and Yeh's team of investigators [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0060), [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0065), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0070), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0075), [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0080) published the very first study on HEAs. HEAs are dependent on the novel configurational entropy maximization alloy design concepts, which offer great versatility for developing diverse materials [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0085), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0090), [[19]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0095), [[20]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0100), [[21]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0105), [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0110). In contrast with present-day physical-metallurgy systems, the advanced mixing entropy in HEAs favors the creation of solid-solution phases such as body-centered cubic (BCC), face-centered cubic (FCC), hexagonal closed packing (HCP), and multiphases rather than [intermetallic compounds](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/intermetallics "Learn more about intermetallic compounds from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IMCs) and perhaps other intricate phases [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0115), [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0120). Melting by arc, [Bridgman method](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/crystal-growth-from-melt "Learn more about Bridgman method from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of solidification, atomization, cladding by laser, manufacturing by [additive technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about additive technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), alloying by mechanical systems, powder metallurgical route, and incorporating vapor state elements using deposition of vapor phase and atomic layers, and sputtering are some of the production processes that may be utilized to create HEAS [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0125), [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0130), [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0135), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0140), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0145), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0150). Subsequently, investigations have taken place on a variety of HEAs, notably FeNiMnCoCr [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0155), AlCoCrFeNi2.1 [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0160), CoCrFeNi Al 0.6[[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0165), CrCoFeNiNb [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0170), CoCrFeNiCu [[35]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0175), AlCrTiVZr [[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0115), and CrTiNbZrAl x[[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0180), among others. In this work, the studies on the HEAs (typically CoCrFeNiMn and Al x CoCrFeNi HEAs) are briefly reviewed. A basic explanation of the four core effects of HEAs is given. Discussion is held on microstructure and mechanical properties of HEAs. The processing routes for manufacturing of HEAs(arc melting, bridgman solidification, mechanical alloying and vapour deposition) are presented briefly.The influence of heat treatment on [mechanical behavior](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/mechanical-property "Learn more about mechanical behavior from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and microstructure of HEAs is presented. The simulation approach of CALPHAD modeling for designing of HEAs is discussed briefly. The future scope for research and development of HEAs in aeroengine applications is briefed.

2. Definition of HEAs
---------------------

Since there are different interpretations of HEAs dependent on composition and entropy, it is unclear whether multi-elemental alloys could be categorized as HEAs.

### 2.1. Composition based definition

According to compositional definitions, HEAs are alloys with an atomic percent range of 5 to 35% with at least five major elements [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0185), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0190). HEAs don't necessarily be equimolar or substantially equimolar in order to equalize various material properties like toughness, [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ductility, strength, creep etc[[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0195), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0200). Even small elements are conceivable in them. The atomic proportion of any minor element present in HEAs is <5% [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0205), [[42]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0210). This composition-based definition alone specifies the constituent concentrations; it places no restrictions on the entropy's magnitude. Therefore, this idea does not need the existence of a single-phase SS.

### 2.2. Entropy based definition

The degree of configurational entropy of mixing of such alloys determines the concept of HEAs in accordance with entropy. Hence, it distinguishes between high EAs (S SS,ideal greater than 1.61R) with more than 05 primary elements of alloying, medium EAs (0.69R<S SS,ideal<1.61R) with two to 04 primary elements of alloying, and low EAs (S SS,ideal<0.69R) with classic alloys. Here, R is the gas constant, and S SS,ideal is the overall configurational molar entropy in an idealized solid solution [[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0215). The words multi-component alloys (MCAs), multi-principal element alloys (MPEAs), concentrated solid solution alloys (CSSs) [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0220), complex concentrated alloys (CCAs) [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0225), and metal buffets (MBs) [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0230) are often used to designate to these alloys.

3. Four-core effects of HEAs
----------------------------

Owing to the fact that HEAs are multi-principal element systems, they exhibit four main effects: greater entropy, extreme distortion of lattice, slow diffusion and cocktail effects. It sets HEAs apart from classic alloys. [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#f0010)[[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0015) offers a illustration of key effects in HEAs.

### 3.1. Greater entropy

The Greater entropy effect constitutes the initial notable HEA effect. The evolution of solution phases may be advanced because of this, and the microstructure can become much relatively simple than initially thought. Due to solution hardening, this aspect may increase the strength and ductility of solution phases. The term “entropy” refers mostly to configurational entropy linked to Gibb's free energy. The “greater entropy effect” prevents the emergence of brittle [intermetallic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/intermetallics "Learn more about intermetallic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with extremely ordered structures. Greater configurational entropy is correlated with it [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0235). The phase would stay stable because, in accordance with the Greater entropy theory, the entropy would be much significant and free energy would subsequently decrease. With the simultaneous enhancement in the solid solubility of alloying constituents, the amount of phases in the HEAs may also be shown to be diminishing. It may be understood using Gibbs free energy formulation, G=H-TS, that expresses Gibbs free energy and the system's temperature, enthalpy, and entropy in sequence. If the temperature is elevated enough, entropy would prevail, and a phase would become prominent at greater entropy [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0240), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0245). A metal has a greater entropy when it's melted compared to when it remains solid, and Richard's rule asserts that the distinction between both states is relatively similar to the gas constant R. The establishment of simple solid solutions is stimulated by the greater entropy effect in HEAs, which causes a substantial reduction in the total number of phases compared to the maximum number (i.e., n+1, where n is the number of constituents) predicted by phase rule of Gibbs'. Therefore, the microstructure is simpler than initially thought, increasing the possibility that it may display superior properties [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0250). Earlier, it was argued that the ability to stabilize a solid solution, the greater entropy impact outweighed other factors like atomic radii and packing density [[51]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0255). Nonetheless, it ought to be outlined that the AlCoCrCuFeNi alloy system illustrates that HEAsmay still respond brittlely [[52]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0260), [[53]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0265). [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#f0015) illustrates the strength-ductility trade-off [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0190); high-strength alloys frequently have poor ductility, and inversely. Nonetheless, certain HEAs do permit circumventing the strength-ductility trade-off due to its diverse principal element constitution. They encompass extraordinary mechanical properties such excellent fracture toughness at extremely low temperatures [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0040), [[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0285), elevated temp mechanical performance [[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0270), [[55]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0275), [[56]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0280), or specific stiffness [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0245), [[52]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0260), among others. Some alloy theories give [superparamagnetism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/superparamagnetism "Learn more about superparamagnetism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and other interesting characteristics [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S258929912300040X#b0300).

It was once believed that the different elements making up lattice of crystal with different atomic radii were the cause of the lattice deformation effect because they might induce local atom deflections related to their locations in diluted alloys. Properties and Preparation of High Entropy Alloys Xiang Yin1 and Shuqiong Xu1* 1School of Mechanical and Vehicle Engineering, Linyi University, Shandong, China Abstract. As a consequence of multi-components, the high entropy alloys embodied serious cocktail party effect and lattice distortion. So high entropy alloys have high strength and hardness possess many comprehensive properties such as thermostability and corrosion resistance.
Because of excellent mechanical properties and chemical properties, high entropy alloys have immeasurable potential of development. This paper mainly introduces the properties, preparations and applications of high entropy alloys, and finally summarizes them.
1 Introduction From the traditional concepts’ perspective, excessive components will give rise to degradation of integrated performance. After the proposal of Yeh. J .W [1]in 2004 there are so many excellent researchers carrying out extensive researches and explorations . A lot of system have been developed, such as AlxFeCoNiCrTi[2]and AlTiFeNiCuCrx[3], mainly composed of transition elements, which possesses simple crystal structures. Till now, some high entropy alloys has already received a range of generalizations and applications for the cheap costs of development.
2 The definition and crystal structures 2.1 Definition According to the theory of Yeh. W. L, high entropy alloys generally consist of more than five main elements. The proportions between principal components are mole ratio or close to the mole ratio. And the contention of main elements is in the range of 5%-35%, while the content of minor elements is less than 5%.
2.2 Crystal Structures In the traditional Gibbs phase rule, the number of equilibrium phases equals to N+1 in the system of alloys with N kinds of main elements. The number of high entropy alloys is not * Corresponding author: xushuqiong11@sina.com © The Authors, published by EDP Sciences. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License 4.0 (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).
MATEC Web of Conferences 142, 03003 (2018) https://doi.org/10.1051/matecconf/201814203003 ICMAE2017 less than 5%. In this concept, high entropy alloys are bound to produce a wide variety of intermetallic compounds, which increased great extent brittleness and reduced the mechanical properties. Fortunately, instead of intricate intermetallic compounds, high entropy alloys formed some simple and common crystal structures such as BCC, FCC, HCP, even the non-crystalline structures [4]. This phenomenon is called high entropy effect [5], which constrained the formation of intermetallic compounds, while forming simple crystal structures.
3 Properties 3.1 High strength and high hardness.
On the one hand, the alloy will have an intensive lattice distortion effect as a result of uneven atomic radius of various elements. This kind of effect can hinder dislocation movement, which leads to very high strength and hardness. Fig.1. is a schematic diagram of the lattice distortion of high entropy alloy containing Cr. On the other hand, the "cocktail effect"[6] of high entropy alloys intensified the interaction of various alloying elements.
Consequently, the high entropy alloy can reflect the comprehensive properties of all kinds of alloy elements. Therefore, the high entropy alloy reflects composite effect improving the strength and hardness.
Fig. 1. Schematic diagram of lattice distortion of high entropy alloy containing Cr As is shown in many experiments, high entropy alloys have higher high hardness. Guo Jianbin[7] melted out of four groups of high entropy alloy, ZrTiHfVNb, ZrTiHfNbMo, ZrTiHfVTa and TaMoWNbZr, and he carried out a compression experiment and measured the values of yield strength, which are respectively 1152MPa, 1134MPa, and 1089MPa.
However, with the increases in the experimental load, the three kinds of alloys were soon broken, and the measured plastic strain is lower. The value of yield strength of ZrTiHfVNb is only 836MPa, but the alloy showed excellent plasticity and the plastic strain reached 55%.
3.2 Corrosion resistance For one thing, because the crystal structure of high entropy alloy tends to be simplified, the corrosion resistance of high entropy alloys is superior to that of plenty of widely used stainless steel. For another thing, lots of high entropy alloy are dendritic segregation structures[8], which are composed of simple surface structure and body structure. And there are many disordered structures and nanometer particles in dendritic segregation structures.
2 MATEC Web of Conferences 142, 03003 (2018) https://doi.org/10.1051/matecconf/201814203003 ICMAE2017 The appearance of these structures has greatly increased the corrosion resistance of high entropy alloys. For example, high entropy alloy Cu0.5NiAlCoCrFeSi at room temperature exhibited better corrosion resistance than 304 and stainless steel in 1mol/L NaCl and 0.
5mol/L H2SO4 solutions[9].
3.3 Thermal stability Entropy is the physical quantity of the system chaos in thermodynamics. The greater entropy means the greater the degree of chaos. According to the function of Gibbs free energy, the entropy of the system (S), temperature (T), enthalpy (H) satisfied the following mathematical relations: G=H-TS (1) It can be known that higher entropy value makes the system more stable. And the high temperature can also reduce the free energy of the system, so the high entropy alloys have good thermal stability.
4 The preparation 4.1 Magnetron sputtering.
Magnetron sputtering[10] is to use certain high energy particle to bombard the surface of a specific material. The process is shown in Fig. 2., argon is placed in a magnetron sputtering device. Under the action of strong electric field, argon wills ionization argon ion and electrons. Then, ions are accelerated toward the cathode in the electric field to bombard target surface with high energy, resulting the occurrence of sputtering target. Therefore, placed in a magnetic field and an electric field with orthogonal distribution, the target material will emit secondary electrons, in which case, the target material will ionize more ions to continue to bombard the target; the target will be sputter deposition to the substrate surface to form a thin film.
Fig. 2. Magnetron sputtering illustrative diagram The FeCrCoNiMn high entropy alloy thin films were deposited on 304 stainless steel substrates and Si (100) substrates by magnetron sputtering at Lanzhou University[11]. The results show that the films are uniform and amorphous in a certain sputtering power. But when the sputtering power reaches a certain value, it will get a very rough film and the surface density decreases. With the increasing of sputtering power, the thin films will 3 MATEC Web of Conferences 142, 03003 (2018) https://doi.org/10.1051/matecconf/201814203003 ICMAE2017 become smaller, and the amorphous structure will also be transformed into crystal structure.
Therefore, the sputtering power can directly affect the properties of the film.
4.2 Vacuum arc melting.
Here are the brief descriptions of the process of Vacuum arc melting. Firstly, a certain proportion of the pure metal and then placed in the pot, in the vacuum furnace and the air out of the repeated charge of protective gas. Then, the arc discharge is formed between the electrode with the high current and the pot, so that the temperature can be raised so high in a short time, which can make sure the alloy, can be uniformly melted in a short time. And the final step is water cooled casting. The advantages of the method are that the refined alloy has high purity, can prevent pollution, and can refine the metal with high melting point[12].
With Vacuum arc melting, Cu0, 5AlFeNiCrTi and CuAlFeNiCrTi are melted by Gao Peng[13]. The microstructure, crystal structure, mechanical properties and corrosion resistance of the two alloys were analyzed by a series of experiments. It was found that these two kinds of alloy have a simple crystal structure with excellent mechanical properties and corrosion resistance.
4.3 Laser cladding Laser cladding is a new technology of smelting high entropy alloys in recent years. And it broke through the dimensional limitation of traditional high entropy alloys. And the micro structure of alloys is uniform and compact with fewer micro defects [14].
Qiu Xingwu chose the matrix material of Q235 steel, coated with high entropy alloy Al2CrFeCoCuNixTi (x=0, 0. 5, 1, 1. 5, 2) with different nickel contents. He found out that the structure of these alloys are simple BCC+FCC through the XRD[15].
5 Applications 5.1 Application of cutting tools.
Comparing the friction and wear behavior of high entropy coating tool and ordinary high speed steel[16]. When the external load and rotational speed are fixed, the friction of the high entropy alloy coated cutting tool is always lower than that of the ordinary high speed steel. When the tool is rotating at high speed, the higher the friction coefficient is, the higher the friction resistance will be. At this time the chip is very easy to stick knife, and it will scratch the surface of the material, which will reduce the surface finish of the material.
Accordingly, the high entropy alloy coating on the surface of the cutting tool can reduce the friction force in the cutting process, which will prolong the tool life and improve the surface quality of the machined surface. Finally, because of high strength and hardness, high entropy alloy coating can be used in high speed cutting.
5.2 Application of hydrogen storage.
Many researches and experiments show that some high entropy alloys have excellent hydrogen storage function. Qiu Jianping studied the hydrogen storage properties of these three kinds of high entropy alloys, which are respectively ScTiVCrCo, ScTiVCrNi, ScTiVCrCu. The results show that the maximum hydrogen absorption capacity of the three alloys is respectively 1.76%, 2.19%, and 2. 56%. With the increase of the number of 4 MATEC Web of Conferences 142, 03003 (2018) https://doi.org/10.1051/matecconf/201814203003 ICMAE2017 hydrogen absorption, the amount of hydrogen absorption will be reduced correspondingly [17].
5.3 Application of chemical corrosion.
Because of eminent corrosion resistance, the high entropy alloy can be applied to all kinds of corrosive working circumstances such as the bottom of ocean ship, the occasions which are prone to electrochemical reaction and various chemical vessels, etc..
5.4 High load and high impact applications.
High entropy alloys can be used to produce high quality golf hitting surfaces, high hardness steel pipe and turbine blade, which work in high load and high circumstances. High entropy alloys can also be used for national defense science and technology, such as the track of tanks, artillery shells, etc.

enriched_points.expanded_content: High-entropy alloys (HEAs) exhibit remarkable tensile strength due to their unique microstructure and severe lattice distortion, enhancing solid solution strength beyond conventional alloys.
Despite their strength, HEAs maintain high ductility, allowing for significant deformation before failure, which is crucial for applications requiring toughness under stress.
HEAs demonstrate superior wear and corrosion resistance due to their complex microstructure and diverse elemental composition, outperforming traditional alloys in harsh environments.
HEAs significantly surpass traditional alloys in strength-to-weight ratios, fracture toughness, and thermal stability, making them ideal candidates for advanced engineering applications.
